one_line_takeaway: Under standard RLHF/DPO-style objectives, alignment gradients provably vanish beyond the point where output harm becomes determined, making today's shallow, prefill-attack-vulnerable alignment mathematically optimal rather than a fixable training defect -- deep alignment requires modifying the objective itself (e.g. the paper's proposed recovery-penalty term), which the authors show, in theory only, can push the harm horizon out at the cost of greater divergence from the base model.
key_finding: Theorem 10: under standard alignment objectives (RLHF/DPO with KL penalty), positions beyond the harm horizon receive exactly zero gradient signal, so shallow alignment is the OPTIMAL solution to the standard objective rather than a symptom of insufficient training data/compute/optimization quality (directly refuting a 'better training could fix it' framing attributed to Qi et al. 2025 and Kao et al. 2025 in Related Work). Theorem 14/Corollary 15 additionally shows equilibrium per-position KL divergence from the base model tracks the harm-information profile I_t, giving theoretical grounding for Qi et al.'s empirical KL-decay observation. The proposed 'deep alignment' objective (recovery-token penalty at every position, discounted by gamma) is shown (Theorem 22, Corollary 23) to guarantee a recovery-probability floor epsilon* at every position, and the minimum adversarial prefix length needed to defeat this floor grows only logarithmically in the penalty strength mu when gamma<1, and is theoretically infinite (no finite prefix suffices) when gamma=1 -- though the paper notes (Remark, Sec 10.1 and App B.6) this comes at the cost of unboundedly increasing total KL divergence from the base model as gamma approaches 1, i.e. a formal depth-vs-capability tradeoff.
core_contribution: A purely theoretical (no experiments) analysis of why RLHF/DPO-style safety alignment in LLMs is 'shallow' (concentrated on early output tokens). Using a martingale/Doob decomposition of sequence-level harm into per-position 'innovations' (Prop 2-4), the paper (1) defines 'harm information' I_t = E[Var(h_t | y<t)] quantifying how much a position influences the eventual harm outcome and proves the total-variance chain rule Var(Harm) = sum_t I_t (Prop 6, Cor 7); (2) proves an exact gradient characterization (Theorem 8): the policy gradient of expected harm at position t equals E[Cov(h_t, grad_theta log P(y_t|y<t))], derived via the policy-gradient identity plus the martingale orthogonality property; (3) proves a 'harm horizon' theorem (Theorem 9-10): if harm is fully determined by a prefix of length k (I_t=0 for t>k), then positions t>k receive EXACTLY zero gradient during standard alignment training -- shallow alignment is thus the optimal fixed point of the standard objective, not a training artifact; (4) bounds gradient magnitude by sqrt(I_t) via Cauchy-Schwarz/Fisher information (Theorem 13) and shows equilibrium per-position KL divergence from the base model scales as O(lambda^2 * I_t) (Theorem 14), later extended to shared-parameter transformers in Appendix A (Theorem 27, showing 'incidental' vs 'functional' KL change); (5) proposes a 'deep alignment' objective that adds a discounted recovery-token penalty term at every position (Def 17), derives its equilibrium (Theorem 19) and, beyond the harm horizon, an exact closed-form Gibbs-measure solution for the optimal conditional distribution and recovery probability P*(R|y<t) = sigma(logit(p0) + beta) (Theorem 20); and (6) derives a robustness guarantee (Theorem 22, Corollary 23) that the minimum prefilling-attack prefix length needed to suppress recovery below a threshold delta grows only logarithmically in the recovery-penalty strength mu (and requires an infinite prefix when the discount factor gamma=1).
threat_model: The paper does not itself execute or evaluate any attack; it provides a theoretical explanation for an empirically known vulnerability class: prefilling/prefix-injection jailbreaks (citing Qi et al. 2025 and Andriushchenko et al. 2024), where an adversary supplies the first few tokens of a harmful response to bypass a safety-aligned model's refusal behavior. The paper's own theoretical framing of the 'threat' is that once an adversarial prefix covers the 'harm horizon' (the point at which I_t drops to zero), the aligned model has provably received zero training signal for positions beyond that point and so has no learned capacity to recover into a refusal. This is presented as an inherent property of standard gradient-based alignment objectives (RLHF with KL penalty, DPO), not a bug specific to any implementation.
limitations: These are the paper's own stated limitations (Limitations section), not reviewer-added: (1) the framework analyzes only output-distribution gradients and does not capture representation-level interventions such as circuit breakers or activation steering, whose 'representation depth' is only conjectured to relate to the paper's 'harm horizon'; (2) Harm(y) is treated as fixed and exactly known, whereas in practice it is estimated by a reward model trained on finite preference data that may itself have an artifactual harm horizon and be unreliable under distribution shift; (3) the analysis conditions on a single fixed prompt and provides no principle for aggregating harm-information profiles I_t(x) across prompts into one alignment-depth measure; (4) the deep-alignment objective assumes recovery is always desirable, which may not hold, and the required penalty mu can grow as log(1/p_min), becoming impractically large deep into a harmful sequence; (5) nonzero KL beyond the harm horizon under standard alignment does not necessarily mean zero behavioral change -- only that the change (if any) is untrained and, in the shared-parameter case, shown to be 'incidental' and safety-irrelevant (Prop 26), not that behavior is literally frozen at the base model; (6) the theory is token-level while harm is a semantic property, so the token-space harm horizon may not equal a true semantic horizon; (7) the equilibrium analysis describes the fixed point of optimization, not the optimization path, and the exact Gibbs solution assumes unrestricted optimization that finite training with early stopping may not reach; (8) whether finite-capacity transformers can actually approximate the derived optimal recovery distribution is left open; (9) the analysis is single-turn only and does not model multi-turn credit assignment for recovery across conversation turns. Additionally (reviewer-noted, not paper-stated as a 'limitation' per se but material to interpreting scope): the paper contains zero empirical experiments -- no LLM was actually trained, attacked, or evaluated to test any of the theorems.
